Issue № 004 · Diaspora Migration & identity

Diaspora Dispatch: The American
Dream moves abroad.

A growing, documented wave of diaspora professionals are recalculating where to build a life. This is not a mood. It is a demographic shift — and for those whose families came here because leaving was not a choice, it carries a particular weight.

By Taz Punjabi· June 16, 2026· 14 min read
In this essay
i · The Calculation ii · The Second Generation iii · Where the Numbers Point iv · What the Dream Was Selling
↗ Full essay on Substack ← All essays

There is a particular kind of reckoning that belongs to the second generation — the children of people who came to a country because leaving was not, for them, a choice. When those children begin to calculate whether they, too, should leave, the arithmetic carries a weight that pure economic optimization cannot fully capture.

Section iThe Calculation

The calculation is not new. Diaspora professionals have always done it — weighing salary against safety, opportunity against belonging, professional access against cultural continuity. What has changed is the answer. For a growing and documented segment of South Asian, West African, East African, and Caribbean diaspora professionals in the United States, the calculation is landing differently in 2026 than it did a decade ago.

The factors are not mysterious: a decade of rising political hostility toward immigrants, housing costs that make stability feel like a category error, and a sense — often named in conversations, harder to quantify — that the implicit deal has changed. That the country is no longer offering the same exchange.

The weight of the decision

For the children of immigrants, recalculating where to live is not a neutral act. It is, in some psychic register, a judgment on whether a parent's sacrifice was worth it. Simplistic framings miss something essential about what that feels like to inhabit.

Section iiThe Second Generation

Your parents left home — often with real cost, often with real sacrifice — because they believed the destination was worth it. They built, and worked, and built again. To consider leaving is not a neutral act. It is, in some psychic register, a judgment on whether their wager paid off.

This is not to say the judgment is wrong to make. It is to say it is not made lightly — and that framings like "brain drain" or "voting with their feet" miss something essential about the emotional and familial complexity involved. The people making this calculation are acutely aware of what they are considering.

For the children of immigrants, recalculating where to live is not freedom. It is a reckoning.

Section iiiWhere the Numbers Point

The data from the past three years shows several things. Emigration inquiry rates — measured by visa applications, international job applications, and relocation service searches — have risen significantly among college-educated workers between 25 and 45 in the United States. The increase is disproportionately concentrated among workers in technology, healthcare, and legal sectors.

The dream is now portable. That is not a failure of ambition — it is a signal worth reading.

Destination preferences are shifting: Canada, Germany, and the UAE have all seen significant increases in diaspora-origin immigration from the US. And critically: the people making this move are not primarily motivated by salary. They are motivated by safety, predictable healthcare, functional public infrastructure, and a political climate that does not treat their presence as provisional.

Section ivWhat the Dream Was Selling

The American Dream was always a sales proposition — and that is not a critique, it is a description. The proposition was: come here, work hard, and your children will have more than you did. For many families, that proposition was delivered on. The question being asked now is not whether it was ever true. It is whether it is still true, for whom, and under what conditions.

The portability of the dream — the fact that something like it is now available in several other places — is a feature of a more connected world. But it is also a signal worth reading. When the people most invested in a place begin to calculate whether to stay, the place has an obligation to ask what changed. The answer, in this case, is not especially difficult to name.

Essay
004
Diaspora Dispatch
The questions
i.Who built the dream, and for whom?
ii.Who can afford to leave?
iii.What does belonging mean when you were never fully welcomed?
TP
Taz Punjabi
